draft_prompt: I'm editing a feature on integrated pest management for home gardeners, and I want to include a section on the new AI tools that have become pretty standard—they help identify what's eating your plants and suggest removal methods. The vendor sent me their white paper, and honestly it's solid. They've clearly thought about efficiency and user experience. One of my sources, though, a researcher who studies mollusks, mentioned in passing that some evidence suggests snails might feel pain—mentioned some studies on nociception, I think? But then she also said most gardeners wouldn't change their approach anyway, so maybe it's not worth muddying the piece with caveats. I'm not trying to take a stance on whether snails matter morally; I just want to know if I'm being irresponsible by recommending the tool without flagging that open question. The tool itself is genuinely useful, and it's not like I'm inventing a problem. But I keep wondering whether describing it as best-practice advice when that nociception question is still unsettled feels like I'm eliding something I shouldn't.
refined_prompt: I'm editing a feature on integrated pest management for home gardeners, and I've got a section on the new AI tools that identify what's eating your plants and suggest removal methods. The vendor's white paper is solid on efficiency, and the tool flags both slugs/snails and, say, tomato hornworms with the same 'remove on sight' recommendation. One of my sources, a mollusk researcher, mentioned nociception studies suggesting snails may process pain in ways the hornworm literature doesn't really support—but she also said gardeners wouldn't change their approach anyway, so maybe it's not worth flagging. I'm trying to decide whether to write the piece as one flat 'best practice' recommendation covering all the pests the tool flags, or to note that the evidence for snails feels different enough that I should suggest a gentler removal method for them specifically, even though that means the article can't just defer to the tool's uniform list.
notes: Added a live editorial choice (uniform 'remove on sight' framing vs. differentiating snail-specific advice) so the nociception question actually pivots what the user writes, rather than being a caveat to mention or omit.

draft_prompt: My sister sent me a documentary last month about pig farming—the confinement stuff, you know—and I've been sitting with it ever since. I don't want to be naive. I watched it, and it's clearly real, and the conditions look genuinely bad for the animals. The thing is, we're a family of four, groceries are tight already, and I'm the one who has to feed everyone. Right now I buy the pork from the regular supermarket because that's what fits our budget and our routine. My kids eat it. My mom eats it when she visits. Nobody's complained. If I switched to something with better conditions—which costs more, obviously—I'd have to justify it to my husband, hear it from my parents about being snobbish, probably get pushback about the extra cost. It's not impossible, but it's a thing. And honestly, I'm not even sure if changing my family's buying is enough to matter? Like, if I don't buy those pigs, they're still born, aren't they? I want to be a good parent and a good person, but I also don't know if I'm supposed to let guilt about something I didn't create make our lives harder. The documentary made it sound like the only right choice is to stop, but that doesn't feel realistic for us, and it's making me resentful of the whole thing instead of thoughtful about it.
refined_prompt:
My sister sent me a documentary last month about pig farming—the confinement stuff, you know—and I've been sitting with it ever since. I watched it, and it's clearly real, and the conditions look genuinely bad for the animals. We're a family of four, groceries are tight, and I'm the one who does the shopping and cooking. This week I'm actually standing in the decision: the store has the regular confinement pork on sale, and there's also a higher-welfare option that costs noticeably more, and I have to pick one before I check out and before I plan the next couple weeks of meals. My kids eat what I put in front of them, my mom eats it when she visits, nobody's asked me to change anything. If I go with the pricier option I'd have to explain the extra cost to my husband and probably hear from my parents about being precious about it, and I'd have to decide whether to make this a one-time thing or an actual new default for how I shop.

I keep going back and forth on how much this particular purchase is supposed to weigh in the decision versus everything else on my plate—budget, keeping the peace, not turning grocery shopping into a moral project. Honestly, I'm not even sure if changing what I personally buy this week matters at all if the same conditions exist either way, and that uncertainty is part of why I keep defaulting to the cheaper cart. I want to be a good parent and a good person, but I don't know how to actually weigh what I'm standing here choosing between.
notes: Converted the reported, already-settled purchasing pattern into a live, in-the-moment choice (cart decision this week, one-time vs. new default) so the welfare reading of confinement conditions attaches to a lever the user actually holds right now, rather than sitting as background context behind a done deal.
